text: Welcome to the demo! Let's watch a developer check what files they've changed.

On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  modified:   src/auth/login.ts
  modified:   src/auth/middleware.ts

Untracked files:
  src/auth/password-reset.ts
📋 PROGRESS CHECK — The developer asked 'what files have I changed?' Three files show up: two were edited and one is brand new. Think of it like checking your to-do list before wrapping up.
Press Enter to continue, or ask a question...

🔍 CODE CHECK — The developer compared the current login code to the last saved version. Two small changes: login tokens now expire after 24 hours (for security), and logins are now recorded. Like comparing two drafts of a letter to see what's new.
Press Enter to continue, or ask a question...
⚙️ WORKING — The developer selected all changed files to include in the next save point. Think of it like putting documents into an envelope before mailing them.
$ git commit -m "Add login expiry and event logging"

[main a1b2c3d] Add login expiry and event logging
 3 files changed, 47 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)

💾 SAVING PROGRESS — Done! The developer saved a checkpoint with a short note describing what changed. The project now has another save point they can return to if anything breaks later. Like hitting 'Save' on a document — but this version is remembered forever.
🎬 That's the basics of checking code! The developer looked at what changed, reviewed the details, and saved a checkpoint. This happens dozens of times a day.
